questions.question: Explain the difference between Classful and Classless IP addressing. Why was classless addressing introduced?
keyPoints: Classful uses fixed A, B, C, D, E classes with default masks
Classless (CIDR) uses variable-length subnet masks
Classful wastes IP addresses - Class C too small, Class B too large
CIDR allows efficient IP allocation and reduces routing table size

questions.question: What is subnetting? Explain with an example how you would subnet a Class C network into 4 equal subnets.
keyPoints: Subnetting divides network into smaller networks
Borrow bits from host portion for subnet ID
Example: 192.168.1.0/26 with /26 mask
4 subnets each with 62 usable hosts (2^6 - 2)

questions.question: Describe the Count-to-Infinity problem in Distance Vector routing. How is it prevented?
keyPoints: Occurs when routers have inconsistent routing information
Count increases indefinitely due to routing loops
Prevention methods: Split horizon, Poison reverse, Maximum hop count
RIP sets maximum hop count to 15 to limit this problem

questions.question: Explain the working of Dijkstra's algorithm for finding shortest path in a network.
keyPoints: Initialize source node distance = 0, others = ∞
Select node with minimum distance, mark as permanent
Update distances of adjacent nodes through current node
Repeat until all nodes are visited

questions.question: What are the advantages of Link State routing over Distance Vector routing?
keyPoints: Faster convergence than Distance Vector
Each router has complete network topology
Less susceptible to routing loops
More scalable for larger networks
Uses cost metric instead of hop count

questions.question: Explain the purpose of NAT and list its advantages.
keyPoints: NAT maps private IP addresses to public IP address
Enables multiple devices to share single public IP
Provides basic security by hiding internal network
Conserves IPv4 address space
Allows flexible network design

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic routing? Discuss their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Static: Manual route configuration; Dynamic: Automatic route learning
Static: Low overhead, predictable; Dynamic: Adaptive to changes, scalable
Static: No routing overhead, more secure; Dynamic: Consumes bandwidth, complex
Static suitable for small networks; Dynamic for large networks

questions.question: Compare and contrast Distance Vector and Link State routing protocols. Give examples of each and explain their working principles.
keyPoints: Distance Vector: Exchanges entire routing table with neighbors periodically
Link State: Exchanges only link state information with all routers
RIP is distance vector using hop count; OSPF is link state using cost
Distance Vector suffers from count-to-infinity; Link State converges faster
Link State requires more memory and processing; Distance Vector is simpler
Split horizon and poison reverse prevent loops in Distance Vector
Link State uses Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest path calculation

questions.question: Explain the concept of IP addressing and subnetting. Given the IP address 192.168.10.50/26, determine: (i) Network address (ii) Broadcast address (iii) First usable host (iv) Last usable host (v) Number of usable hosts.
keyPoints: /26 means subnet mask 255.255.255.192 (2 bits borrowed from host)
Block size = 256 - 192 = 64
Network address: 192.168.10.0 (50 falls in 0-63 range)
Broadcast address: 192.168.10.63 (last address in block)
First usable: 192.168.10.1
Last usable: 192.168.10.62
Usable hosts: 2^(32-26) - 2 = 62

questions.question: Describe the routing process in the internet. Explain how routing tables are used and what is meant by 'longest prefix match'.
keyPoints: Routers examine destination IP address in packet
Lookup routing table for matching network entry
Longest prefix match selects most specific route
If no match, use default route (0.0.0.0/0)
Routing table contains: Destination network, Subnet mask, Next hop, Interface
Default route used when no specific route matches
Example: /24 route preferred over /16 for same destination

questions.question: Explain the BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) and its role in internet routing. How does it differ from interior gateway protocols like OSPF?
keyPoints: BGP is path vector protocol for inter-AS routing
Uses AS_PATH attribute to prevent loops
TCP port 179 for reliable communication
Policy-based routing using route attributes
Interior vs Exterior: OSPF for within AS, BGP for between AS
BGP maintains reachability across thousands of autonomous systems
Uses CIDR for address aggregation to reduce table size
More conservative than IGP due to internet-scale operations
